text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid with transrectal extraction and a sononastomosis.
This one has a slight variation to my normal practice.
I chose to do a lateral to medial in this case, instead of a medial to lateral.
This was because of a couple of reasons. One, the patient was very large body habitus, high BMI, and I had difficulty getting exposure at the root of the IMA.
and two, given the adherence of the bowel and the mesentery to the retroperitoneum, I wasn't getting good elevation, and so I decided to do a lateral to medial.

Another thing that you'll notice in this case is that even after I did the lateral immobilization, I chose not to do a high ligation of the IMA or the superior hemorrhoidal, even though you've heard me say, and you see in a lot of my videos, that that is my standard routine.
I chose to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal in this case as I was able to tell that length was not going to be an issue regardless of taking that vessel.
I will tell you that I found that this case went a lot faster than when I usually do a medial to lateral with a high ligation.
I don't know if it just had to do with this individual case or if it was the technique.

Um, I still maintain that it's important for us as surgeons to be familiar with, um, all iterations of, uh, of the surgery, uh, cause you never know when you might need to fall back on another technique.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, proceeding routinely without any notable deviations from standard technique.
The surgeon performs a limited mobilization of the colonic flexure, advancing towards it without completing a full circumferential dissection.
The surgeon approaches the flexure using a vessel sealer, which serves as both a retractor and provides hemostasis, especially effective for controlling bleeding in distant areas.
The surgeon lifts the mesentery off the left kidney, revealing Giroud's fascia and exposing the tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon assesses the colon to determine the distal and proximal margins for resection, observing the areas where the teniae coli splay out along the colon.
The surgeon marks a section of the colon that is devoid of any diverticula, indicating a smooth surface without protrusions.
The surgeon selects the proximal margin of the colon, identifying and grasping an offending diverticulum with surgical instruments to ensure its removal.
The surgeon begins creating a window in the mesentery under the proximal margin, carefully preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon encounters bleeding on the specimen side and places a sponge to apply tamponade, temporarily controlling the bleeding before moving to another area.
The surgeon returns to the distal margin of the colon and begins working back up towards the proximal margin, focusing on the mesentery and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully preserves the superior hemorrhoidal artery while working on the mesentery, ensuring that the artery remains intact as length is not a concern in this procedure.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and proceeds to dissect along the proximal margin, ensuring clear separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon approaches the area of bleeding with improved exposure, successfully gaining control over the bleeding site.
The surgeon cleans the surgical field, removing excess blood and debris to improve visibility for the camera.
The surgeon traces the mesentery down to the upper rectum and clears the surrounding tissue from the upper rectum.
The patient has an up-to-date colonoscopy. This is only a diverticular disease.
I've decided I'm going to do a transrectal extraction of the specimen. I thought that that would be beneficial for the patient, given the body habitus.
The surgeon assesses the reach and checks the perfusion of the tissue, ensuring adequate blood flow in the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the area using white light to ensure visibility after the indocyanine green (ICG) dye washes out.
The surgeon clears fat from the upper rectum and proceeds to transect the bowel using scissors.

The surgeon transects the proximal margin of the bowel, leaving most of the mesentery attached to the specimen. The specimen appears smaller in caliber compared to typical specimens prepared for extraction.
The surgeon decides to divide the mesentery into three pieces for extraction, despite not needing to amputate it from the specimen.

The surgeon uses a ring clamp to grasp the first piece of the specimen, reaching inside with the index and middle fingers to prevent the bowel mucosa from intussuscepting outward. This technique facilitates the smooth passage of the bowel through the rectal tube. The surgeon successfully extracts all three pieces of the specimen.
The surgeon cleans the surgical area before performing a sewn end-to-end anastomosis using a single layer of V-lock sutures.
The surgeon begins sewing in the posterior midline, carefully taking full thickness bites to ensure the posterior lip is inverted, achieving good apposition of the posterior serosa and mesentery.

The surgeon sews around the outer corner of the bowel, transitioning to an inverting canal technique at that level. The sewing starts from the opposite side to avoid closing the anterior lip excessively, ensuring visibility while sewing around the near corner.
The surgeon begins to canalize the anterior layer of the bowel to invert it, ensuring proper alignment and apposition during the anastomosis.
The surgeon adjusts the sutures, ensuring they are not tied to each other near a corner of the anastomosis.

The surgeon ensures all visible mucosa is tucked inward during the anastomosis, avoiding any outward protrusion. The two ends are tied together, completing the anastomosis. A leak test is performed to check the integrity of the connection.
The surgeon floods the pelvis and uses a rigid scope to inspect the anastomosis. Air insufflation is applied, revealing no visible bubbles, indicating the anastomosis is patent and without bleeding.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical area to ensure good hemostasis, using four eight-millimeter trocars for the procedure.
I hope you enjoyed.
